1: Benjamin’s genealogy begins again — the second, fuller register of this tribe in Chronicles. Benjamin (ben yāmîn, ‘son of the right hand’) holds a paradoxical position in messianic history: from this tribe came Saul the failed king and Paul the apostle. The tribe of the right hand produces both the king whose kingdom fell and the apostle whose proclamation built the church. The right-hand position (‘son of the right’) anticipates the NT’s royal image: Christ seated at the right hand of God (Psalms 110:1; Matthew 26:64; Hebrews 1:3), the ultimate ‘son of the right hand.’
2: Nohah the fourth and Rapha the fifth — completing Benjamin’s five sons. The name Rapha (rāpāʾ, ‘the healer’) carried healing resonance in Israel’s naming culture. Christ as the great healer who came from the covenant people (Matthew 4:23: ‘healing every disease and every affliction’) is the ultimate fulfillment of every healing-name embedded in Israel’s genealogies. YHWH-Rapha (‘the LORD who heals you,’ Exodus 15:26) is the divine name that these genealogical names echo.
3: The sons of Bela, beginning with Addar and Gera. Gera is a recurring Benjaminite clan name: Ehud the left-handed judge was son of Gera (Judges 3:15), and Shimei who cursed David was son of Gera (2 Samuel 16:5). The same clan name produces a deliverer and a curser — the Benjaminite capacity for both faithfulness and rebellion is encapsulated in the onomastic record. Christ is the one who transforms curser into worshipper: he prayed for those who cursed him (Luke 23:34), fulfilling the covenant’s deepest intention.
4: Abishua, Naaman, and Ahoah. The name Naaman (naʿᵃmān, ‘pleasant’) anticipates the Aramean general healed of leprosy by Elisha (2 Kings 5:1-19), who confessed ‘there is no God in all the earth but in Israel.’ Jesus himself cites Naaman as an example of God’s grace extending beyond Israel: ‘there were many lepers in Israel in the time of the prophet Elisha, and none of them was cleansed, but only Naaman the Syrian’ (Luke 4:27). The name appears in Benjamin’s genealogy as a quiet anticipation of the Gentile who would bear it.
5: Gera, Shephuphan, and Huram — completing Bela’s sons. The persistence of clan names across centuries (Shephuphan/Huppim appear in Genesis 46:21 and Numbers 26:39) reflects the covenant community’s commitment to preserving its lineage — the same commitment that makes the NT genealogies of Christ (Matt 1; Luke 3) meaningful: Christ’s ancestry was verifiable through exactly this kind of preserved genealogical record. The incarnation is grounded in history, not myth.
6: The sons of Ehud, heads of ancestral houses at Geba, carried into exile to Manahath. Ehud the Benjaminite judge (Judges 3:15-30) was the left-handed deliverer who killed Eglon. The exile and continuation pattern is the Chronicler’s constant theme: God’s purposes advance through displacement — the same pattern the NT applies to Christ’s death-and-resurrection, the ultimate exile-and-return through which the new creation is inaugurated.
7: Naaman, Ahijah, and Gera — three sub-clan leaders within Ehud’s line, displaced from Geba to Manahath. The forced relocation whose reason is not given reflects YHWH’s superintendence of historical events that appear unexplained. The God who preserved covenant people through such dislocations is the same God who ‘works all things according to the counsel of his will’ (Ephesians 1:11), including the apparently purposeless dislocations of his people’s lives.
8: Shaharaim fathered children in Moab after divorcing his wives — a Benjaminite establishing a family in Moab. The Moab connection runs through David’s ancestry (Ruth the Moabitess) and surfaces again within Benjamin’s genealogy. The presence of Israelite men in Moab (Elimelech in Ruth 1:1-2; Shaharaim here) reflects the geographic porosity between Transjordan and the west-bank tribes. Christ’s genealogy through Ruth demonstrates that the covenant’s reach extends into Moab — and through Christ to all nations.
9: Shaharaim’s seven sons by Hodesh — Jobab, Zibia, Mesha, Malcam, Jeuz, Sachia, and Mirmah. The name Malcam (‘their king’ or ‘Milcom,’ the Ammonite god) within a Benjaminite genealogy reflects the religious syncretism surrounding the covenant community. The covenant people were always embedded in a polytheistic environment; Christ’s redemption includes reclaiming people from the orbit of competing loyalties — transforming those whose names invoke false kings into those who confess the one true King.
10: Jeuz, Sachia, and Mirmah — Shaharaim’s Moabite-born sons as ‘heads of ancestral houses.’ The Chronicler does not exclude these Moabite-born Benjaminites from Israel’s covenant register. This reflects the principle that covenant membership is determined by descent from the covenant patriarch and participation in the community’s identity — a principle fulfilled in Christ, in whom ‘there is neither Jew nor Greek’ (Galatians 3:28) yet all are heirs of Abraham’s promise (Galatians 3:29).
11: Abitub and Elpaal, sons by Hushim — the children of the discarded wife registered in Israel’s covenant roll. The inclusion of a divorced woman’s sons within the genealogical record reflects YHWH’s faithfulness to those marginalized by human failure. Christ’s ministry especially extended to those excluded — the divorced, the socially discarded — precisely because the covenant God had always tracked those whom human society wrote off.
12: Elpaal’s sons — including Shemed who built Ono and Lod. Lod (Lydda) was where Peter healed the paralytic Aeneas: ‘Aeneas, Jesus Christ heals you; rise and make your bed’ (Acts 9:34). The city that Shemed (a Benjaminite) founded in the settlement period became the location of one of the apostle Paul’s fellow-Benjaminite Peter’s healing miracles in the name of Christ. The geographical inheritance first claimed by Benjamin’s sons becomes the setting for the new covenant community’s apostolic activity.
13: Beriah and Shema, heads of houses at Aijalon who drove out the inhabitants of Gath. Aijalon was Joshua’s battle site (Joshua 10:12: ‘Sun, stand still at Gibeon, and moon, in the Valley of Aijalon’) where YHWH fought with miraculous hailstones. The Benjaminites who drove out Gath’s inhabitants from Aijalon reverse the earlier defeat where Gath’s men killed Ephraimite sons (1 Chronicles 7:21). The pattern of defeat followed by victory in the same geographic corridor reflects the covenant narrative’s trajectory: the enemies who struck first are ultimately overcome — as death is overcome in Christ’s resurrection.
14: Ahio, Shashak, and Jeremoth. The name Jeremoth recurs across tribes in Chronicles (a Levitical musician in 1 Chronicles 25:22; men who married foreign wives in Ezra 10). The spread of names across tribes reflects the shared naming culture of Israel that made the twelve tribes one people. Christ’s body is similarly one people with shared identity across every background: ‘one body and one Spirit’ (Ephesians 4:4) whose members share one name.
15: Zebadiah, Arad, and Eder — sons of Beriah. The name Arad recalls the Canaanite city Israel first defeated in the Negev by vow (Numbers 21:1-3: Israel vowed to devote the cities to destruction and YHWH gave them the victory). The naming of an Israelite child Arad suggests the assimilation of conquered place-names into the covenant community’s naming culture — the people carrying their history of YHWH’s victories in their very names.
16: Michael, Ishpah, and Joha — sons of Beriah. Michael (mîḵāʾēl, ‘Who is like God?’) is the name of the archangel-warrior who fights for Israel against spiritual powers (Daniel 10:13,21; Revelation 12:7). The name within Benjamin’s genealogy is a reminder that the human tribe of warriors is sustained by Michael’s cosmic warfare — the spiritual dimension of Israel’s military history that the NT exposes: ‘we do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against... the spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly places’ (Ephesians 6:12), defeated by Christ’s cross (Colossians 2:15).
17: Zebadiah, Meshullam, Hizki, and Heber — sons of Elpaal. Meshullam (mᵉšullām, ‘at peace’ — from šālôm) was among the most common names in the post-exilic period, reflecting the Hebrew longing for šālôm: the wholeness and peace the prophets promised as the Messiah’s gift (Isaiah 9:6: ‘Prince of Peace’; 1 Chronicles 52:7: ‘How beautiful are the feet of him who brings good news, who publishes peace’). Every Meshullam in the genealogical record carries the unspoken petition for the peace Christ finally gives (John 14:27: ‘Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you’).
18: Ishmerai, Izliah, and Jobab — sons of Elpaal. Jobab is the name of an early Edomite king (Genesis 36:33-34) and possibly identifies with Job in some traditions (LXX Job appendix). If the connection to Job is ancient, then a Benjaminite bearing this name carries the memory of the Gentile who declared ‘I know that my Redeemer lives, and at the last he will stand upon the earth’ (Job 19:25) — the resurrection hope grasped by one outside Israel before Israel had articulated it clearly.
19: Jakim, Zichri, and Zabdi — sons of Shimei. Zichri (ziḵrî, ‘my remembrance’) marks YHWH’s remembrance (zeḵer) of his covenant — the basis for every redemptive act from Noah (Genesis 8:1: ‘God remembered Noah’) through the Exodus (Exodus 2:24: ‘God remembered his covenant’) to the incarnation (Luke 1:54-55: ‘He has helped his servant Israel, in remembrance of his mercy’). The name Zichri embedded in Benjamin’s genealogy is a covenant-memory marker pointing toward the God who never forgets his people.
20: Elienai, Zillethai, and Eliel — sons of Shimei. The El-names cluster (‘God is my help,’ ‘shade of God,’ ‘God is exalted’) in one verse reflect the depth of Israel’s naming culture: every name is a statement about YHWH. The accumulation of divine-attribute names is the onomastic equivalent of the divine name’s declaration in Exodus 34:6-7. The God known by his attributes — mercy, grace, faithfulness, justice — is the same God who revealed all his attributes in their fullness in Christ (Colossians 2:9: ‘in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily’).
21: Adaiah, Beraiah, and Shimrath — sons of Shimei. Adaiah (ʿăḏāyāh, ‘YHWH has adorned’) appears in Nehemiah as the name of a returned exile who settled in Jerusalem (Nehemiah 11:5,12) — the post-exilic restoration community included descendants of exactly these Benjaminite clans. The Chronicler’s genealogical project is identity-documentation for that community: you belong to this covenant people, your exile is over. The NT applies the same logic to the church: ‘you are no longer strangers and aliens, but fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God’ (Ephesians 2:19).
22: Ishpan, Eber, and Eliel — sons of Shashak. Eber (ʿēḇer, ‘beyond’ or ‘the other side’) is the ancestor from whom the Hebrews take their name (Genesis 10:24-25; 1 Chronicles 11:14-17). A Benjaminite bearing the name of the proto-ancestor of all Israelites collapses the genealogical distance between clan register and ethnic origin. Christ is ‘the son of Abraham’ (Matthew 1:1) — the fulfillment of the promise given to the Eber-Abraham-Isaac-Jacob-David line that these genealogies trace.
23: Abdon, Zichri, and Hanan — sons of Shashak. Hanan (ḥānān, ‘gracious’) shares the same root as Hannah, John, and Johanan — eight men named Hanan appear in Chronicles and Ezra-Nehemiah, reflecting the name’s popularity as a covenant-grace marker. The grace embedded in these Benjaminite names anticipates the decisive arrival of grace in Christ: ‘For from his fullness we have all received, grace upon grace. For the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ’ (John 1:16-17).
24: Hananiah, Elam, and Antothijah — sons of Shashak. Antothijah links this Benjaminite clan to Anathoth, Jeremiah’s hometown (Jeremiah 1:1). Jeremiah was the prophet of the new covenant (‘I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel,’ Jeremiah 31:31-34, quoted at length in Hebrews 8:8-12 as fulfilled in Christ). A Benjaminite clan name derived from Anathoth ties this tribal register to the greatest prophetic announcement of the covenant that Christ establishes in his blood.
25: Iphedeiah and Penuel — sons of Shashak. Penuel (pᵉnûʾēl, ‘face of God’) is the place where Jacob wrestled with God and was renamed Israel (Genesis 32:30). The name within Benjamin’s genealogy carries the full weight of that encounter. The NT presents the ultimate ‘face of God’ encounter in Christ: ‘For God, who said, “Let light shine out of darkness,” has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ’ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
26: Shamsherai, Shehariah, and Athaliah — sons of Jeroham. Athaliah (ʿᵃṯalyāhû, ‘YHWH is exalted’) is also the name of the queen-usurper who seized the Davidic throne and tried to kill all the royal seed (2 Chronicles 22:10-12) — the only woman to have occupied Judah’s throne. Her attempt to exterminate the Davidic line failed because Jehosheba hid the infant Joash (2 Chronicles 22:11). The line that would produce Christ could not be exterminated by the most determined human effort — a pattern the NT affirms: ‘the gates of Hades will not prevail against’ the community Christ builds (Matthew 16:18).
27: Jaareshiah, Elijah, and Zichri — sons of Jeroham. Elijah (ʾēlîyāhû, ‘my God is YHWH’) is the prophet whose return was awaited as the forerunner of the Messiah (Malachi 4:5), identified by Jesus as fulfilled in John the Baptist (Matthew 11:14; 1 Chronicles 17:12-13). The name Elijah in Benjamin’s genealogy is not predictive, but its pervasive use across Israel’s registers reflects the Elijah tradition’s hold on the messianic hope of the covenant community — the expectation of the forerunner who would prepare the way of the Lord.
28: ‘These were heads of ancestral houses throughout their generations — leading men who lived in Jerusalem.’ The accumulation of Benjaminite families in Jerusalem reflects the deep roots of Benjamin’s connection to the capital. Paul’s identity as a Benjaminite (Philippians 3:5) who carried the gospel from Jerusalem to Rome traces the arc from this Benjaminite residence in Jerusalem to the apostolic commission that Jerusalem-based disciples would execute in Christ’s name across the Roman world (Acts 1:8: ‘you will be my witnesses in Jerusalem, and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth’).
29: The father of Gibeon — Gibeon was a major Benjaminite city that became the location of the bronze altar and tabernacle in David’s day (1 Chronicles 21:29; 2 Chronicles 1:3) and the site where Solomon received wisdom in a dream (1 Kings 3:4-5: YHWH appeared at ‘the most important high place’ and offered Solomon anything he wished). The city where God offered and gave divine wisdom is rooted in Benjamin’s tribal register — a foreshadowing of the one in whom ‘are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge’ (Colossians 2:3), the Christ who is ‘the wisdom of God’ (1 Corinthians 1:24).
30: Abdon the firstborn, Zur, Kish, Baal, and Nadab — the sons of the father of Gibeon. Kish here will become Saul’s father (v33). The name Baal within the Saulide genealogy reflects the religious syncretism the prophets condemned — and the NT fulfills Hosea’s promise that the day will come when Israel calls YHWH ‘my husband’ rather than ‘my Baal’ (Hosea 2:16-17). Christ is the husband of the new covenant community, replacing the Baal-worship with the exclusive covenant loyalty that the law demanded but could not produce.
31: Gedor, Ahio, and Zacher — more sons of the Gibeonite ancestor. The household of Gibeon — Hivites who had obtained a treaty with Israel through deception (Josh 9) but were absorbed into Israel as servants of the sanctuary — is now recorded as progenitors of Saul’s lineage. The Gibeonite inclusion within Israel (Joshua 9:27: ‘woodcutters and water-carriers for the congregation’) is an OT type of the Gentile-inclusion into the covenant community, which Christ’s mission enacts at a cosmic scale.
32: Mikloth fathered Shimeah. They lived in Jerusalem, opposite their kinsmen. The geographic note places real families in a real city, grounding the genealogy in lived geography. The NT’s house-church communities in Rome (Rom 16) display the same pattern: small family groups across a major city constituting the body of Christ. In both cases, the community’s identity is not a metaphysical abstraction but a social reality — families, households, neighbors — in which the covenant life is lived.
33: A shadow: ‘Ner fathered Kish, Kish fathered Saul, and Saul fathered Jonathan, Malchishua, Abinadab, and Eshbaal.’ The Saulide genealogy carries immense Christological weight as the negative type that makes the positive type (David → Christ) legible. Saul was the king Israel demanded (‘give us a king like all the nations,’ 1 Samuel 8:5) — chosen for human-standard qualifications (tall, handsome, militarily imposing). His rejection by YHWH demonstrates that no king chosen by human standards can fulfill the covenant. The contrast between Saul (the law-king, who ultimately brought death) and David (the grace-king, the man after YHWH’s own heart) is the typological contrast Paul unpacks in Romans 5:12-21 between Adam and Christ: the first man brought death; the last man brought life.
34: A type: Jonathan’s son was Meribbaal, and Meribbaal fathered Micah. Meribbaal (2 Sam 9: Mephibosheth) was Jonathan’s son, lame from age five, who was brought to David’s table to eat ‘like one of the king’s sons’ as an act of covenant-loyalty (ḥesed) to Jonathan. This is one of the richest types of grace in the OT: the lame man from the enemy-family, brought to the king’s table and given a permanent place — not for his own merit but for the sake of a covenant his father had made. The type of the messianic feast is explicit in Luke 14:21: ‘bring in the poor and crippled and blind and lame.’ Every gospel invitation to the sinner who has nothing to commend them is Meribbaal carried to David’s table; Christ is the king whose covenant with the Father provides the ground for the invitation.
35: The sons of Micah — Pithon, Melech, Tarea, and Ahaz. The Saulide line continues beyond Meribbaal into further generations. The persistence of the Saulide genealogy even though the dynasty ended serves the Chronicler’s purpose: no Israelite tribe or family is excluded from the covenant register, even those whose royal line failed. The gospel extends the same principle: no human failure disqualifies a person from the covenant invitation, because Christ’s merit stands in place of every failed record (2 Corinthians 5:21: ‘he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God’).
36: Ahaz fathered Jehoadah who fathered Alemeth, Azmaveth, and Zimri — the Saulide line branching generations below Meribbaal. The lineage’s continuation demonstrates that YHWH’s purposes for Benjamin do not depend on the dynasty maintaining political power. The covenant God who maintains the Saulide lineage through the collapse of their kingship is the same God who ‘is able to do far more abundantly than all that we ask or think’ (Ephesians 3:20) — his faithfulness exceeds all dynastic failure.
37: Moza fathered Binea; Rapha, Eleasah, Azel — the Saulide line entering post-monarchic generations. By the Chronicler’s era, this line has continued for perhaps fifteen or more generations after Saul’s death. The survival of the Saulide genealogy demonstrates YHWH’s covenant faithfulness to all twelve tribes, not only to the Davidic line. The Messiah comes from Judah (Genesis 49:10; Micah 5:2) but the covenant people from whom he comes include Benjamin’s preserved lineages — the twelve-tribe people are the soil from which the one Redeemer grows.
38: Azel had six sons — Azrikam, Bocheru, Ishmael, Sheariah, Obadiah, and Hanan. The name Ishmael within Benjamin’s line (a covenant-child tribe bearing the name of the son excluded from the primary covenant line) demonstrates the permeability of Hebrew naming across ethnic memory. Paul’s allegory in Galatians 4:21-31 uses Ishmael and Isaac to represent law versus grace — and both names existed within Israel’s genealogical registers, testimony to a community that held its full ancestral memory while living under the covenant of the Spirit.
39: The sons of Eshek, Azel’s brother — Ulam the firstborn, Jeush the second, Eliphelet the third. Eliphelet (ʾĕlîpeleṭ, ‘God is deliverance’) was also the name of two of David’s sons (1 Chronicles 3:6,8). The shared name across the Saulide and Davidic lines reflects the covenant community’s shared naming vocabulary. Both the rejected dynasty and the chosen dynasty name their sons ‘God is deliverance’ — the declaration is prophetic: the deliverance YHWH promised belongs not to one dynasty but to the covenant purpose that transcends dynasties and is fulfilled in Christ.
40: The chapter closes with Ulam’s sons: mighty warriors, skilled archers, many sons and grandsons — one hundred and fifty in all. The chapter ends not with the Davidic line but with the Saulide line producing warriors and multiplying into a hundred and fifty covenant men. The closing image — the sons of the failed dynasty flourishing as skilled fighters — is a quiet statement about God’s covenant faithfulness: even those whose ancestors failed the kingship test are preserved, enrolled, and numbered in the covenant community. That faithfulness is concentrated and fulfilled in Christ, the Son of David and Son of God, who does not fail where every previous anointed king failed — who is the ‘better David’ whose kingdom is established forever (2 Samuel 7:13; Hebrews 1:8: ‘Your throne, O God, is forever and ever’).
